EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Depression 09F originally formed northwest of Savaii in an expansive area of deep convection and tropical instability, which was reflected well in the rapidly cooling cloud top temperatures noted on IR satellite. TD 09F moved through American Samoa���s area of responsibility (AOR) Thursday morning (Feb 20th) before Fiji Met Services named the system Tropical Storm Vicky (1205pm Thurs afternoon). A Tropical Storm Watch issued Thursday,  was for another developing tropical system northwest of Savaii, 98P, which eventually became known as Tropical Storm Wasi.||The system generated a period of strong winds across American Samoa Thursday morning, between 30 to 50 mph with gusts in the 60s.  At NWS Pago Pago, highest wind observations Thursday morning (around 8am and 9am) were sustained at 46 mph with gusts to 75 mph.||American Samoa Power Authority (ASPA) reported���.25 customers sustained damage to their house and meters,  Power outages reported in villages Tutuila, Manua and Aunuu. Over 300 people were in emergency shelters. 50 people from Onenoa were housed in an emergency shelter, result of their home being washed over by waves. Hawaiian Air announced that they would not operate additional flights through the weekend. Samoa Airways and Talofa Air both suspended flights to American Samoa in the wake of Tropical Storm Vicky and in advance of Tropical Storm Wasi. Pago Pago International Airport extended its closure.||The extensive amounts of damage experienced on American Samoa���s Islands the weekend prior, by the strong winds Saturday night (Feb 15) through Monday (Feb 17), is what likely lessened storm damage experienced with Tropical Storm Vicky Thursday (Feb 20), and eventually during the weekend with Tropical Storm Wasi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near 50 people in Onenoa were housed in an emergency shelter, result of homes being washed over by waves.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely active monsoonal trough in the region of American Samoa, generated an extended period of damaging winds, high surf and flooding rains. Moderate values of mid level shear and stable flow into the eastern portions of the area of responsibility hindered any one tropical circulation from developing within the trough. Therefore, there was no named tropical feature with this event. The extensive amounts of damage noted during this period, is what likely lessened storm damage experienced as Tropical Storm Vicky and Tropical Storm Wasi developed later in the week.||An extended period of sustained winds 35 to 45 mph with frequent gusts 65 to 70 mph, generated widespread damage across the American Samoan Island chain. Strong winds began over Manua Saturday Feb 15th with all of American Samoa experiencing damage Sunday Feb 16th, through early Monday Feb 17th. During this time several reports of roof damage, downed trees, downed power lines and power outages, and rock slides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Pago Pago official observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm near New Zealand produced large swells with long periods that impacted the southern shores of American Samoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of a reef fisherman swept out to sea around 5 PM SST on the 19th. This report was observed on social media and confirmed by the Emergency Operations Center. There were also numerous reports/observations of waves and ocean debris on main roads that impacted south facing shores across the island. Periods of up to 20 seconds were observed by the Aunu'u PacIOOS buoy during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active monsoonal trough lifted across the area from the 17th of January until the week after. This weather event produced heavy showers and strong winds over the territory, which caused landslides and flooded roadways as well as downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Close to midnight on the 17th of January, WSO Pago Pago recorded the highest gust for that day of 46 mph. This wind event caused some damage across Tutuila. On the 17th, the EOC reported a downed tree in Leone that blocked the road and caused traffic. On the 18th, An NWS employee reported downed trees and power lines in Utulei. Another NWS employee reported a tin roof blown off in the village of Nua and Seetaga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to grounds being saturated over a long period of time from the monsoonal trough event that began on the 17th of January, landslides and flooded roadways were easily susceptible during rain events.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee and several callers from the public reported landslides in Amaluia and a minor landslide in Fatu ma Futi. Over a 24-hour period 5.83 inches of rain was recorded at the WSO Pago Pago. This event caused the roads on both lanes to be blocked in Amaluia (there are only two lanes in american samoa), which left residents from villages west of Amaluia stuck westbound until roads were cleared by Public Works later that evening. A minor landslide in Fatu ma Futi blocked only one lane, luckily only a few minor boulders fell and public works was able to clear it up quickly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active monsoonal trough lifted across the area from the 17th of January until the week after. This weather event produced heavy showers and strong winds over the territory, which caused landslides and flooded roadways as well as downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For a 48-hour period (from the 16th to the 18th) the WSO Pago Pago recorded 5.52 inches of rain. This rain event prompted a landslide in Agugulu that blocked the westbound lane.

Strong winds began over Manua Saturday Feb 15th with all of American Samoa experiencing damage Sunday Feb 16th, through early Monday Feb 17th. During this time several reports of roof damage, downed trees, downed power lines and power outages, and rock slides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official NWS observation. Highest winds recorded was 43G68 mph. Family in Fagatogo Village lost entire roof to their home. Family in Aunu'u lost the roof to their home. Roof blown off museum in Fagatogo. Krispy Crunchy Chicken Restaurant lost the back patio. Radio station KHJ had damage to its communication tower due to strong winds. Power outages in Afono and Vatia. Samoa Airways and Talofa Airways cancelled their afternoon flights due to winds. Crops uprooted in Manu'a.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Impacts from the 16th is combined with the 17th as this weather event was from one day to the next. Heavy crop damage. Several homes lost their roofs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf from an extended period of tropical convection 200 to 300 miles northwest of American Samoa and persistent strong northwest flow, generated an extended period of high surf and water piling onshore along American Samoan's north shoreline. Villages along north shores were battered by strong winds and high surf of 20 to 26 feet generating beach erosion, and coastal flooding. Many areas reported debris on roads. Vessels ordered by port admin to anchor outside of harbor due to winds and large surfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough associated with Tropical Cyclone Harold (that was located far south of American Samoa) produced numerous thunderstorms and locally heavy rainfall over the Samoa Island chain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a call from EOC Vanu at 1:00 AM reporting flooding in a low-lying area in the village of Amouli. This event caused flooding in a family home, which prompted the family to evacuate to higher ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASPA reported 25 customers with damages to their house and meters. Power outages scattered across the territory. Flights to American Samoa from Hawaii and Apia suspended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely active monsoonal trough in the region of American Samoa, generated an extended period of damaging winds, high surf and flooding rains. Moderate values of mid level shear and stable flow into the eastern portions of the area of responsibility hindered any one tropical circulation from developing within the trough. Therefore, there was no named tropical feature with this event. The extensive amounts of damage noted during this period, is what likely lessened storm damage experienced as Tropical Storm Vicky and Tropical Storm Wasi developed later in the week.||An extended period of sustained winds 35 to 45 mph with frequent gusts 65 to 70 mph, generated widespread damage across the American Samoan Island chain. Strong winds began over Manua Saturday Feb 15th with all of American Samoa experiencing damage Sunday Feb 16th, through early Monday Feb 17th. During this time several reports of roof damage, downed trees, downed power lines and power outages, and rock slides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total rainfall recorded over a 24 hour period 3.43 inches. Flooding on roads reported in several villages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak convergence over the islands prompted heavy rain showers and over flowing streams from central to eastward villages of Tutuila.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous callers driving towards central to eastward villages of Tutuila (Fagatogo to Leloaloa going east) reported all streams over flowing onto the road and into the ocean. This caused flooding and fast flowing water on roadways, rock slides, and numerous cars to be partially submerged in water. The National Parks rain gauge in Pago Pago recorded 3.29 inches of rain during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low level convergence trough in the area produced heavy rainfall within a 24 hour period, which produced flooded hotspots across the territory.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saturated grounds from days prior to the 17th enhanced flooding in low-lying areas; rainfall reached 2.25 inches of rain early that morning. This event caused stalled cars across the territory and crater-sized potholes in poor drainage areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: American Samoa was at a boundary of a ridge and trough over the weekend of the first week of September. This episode allowed for tight pressure gradient-influenced winds and flooding conditions. Such winds prompted steep seas for mariners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two alia boats battled 24 hours out at sea due to steep wave heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers due to a surface trough in the area caused flooded roadways, mudslides, and streams to overflow in some villages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several NWS employees reported flooded roadways in villages in the harbor area. Overflowing streams and debris on road from a mudslide in the village of Laulii caused a roadblock across both lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Though no advisories or swells were in the area, strong rip currents took the lives of two fishermen in the village of Amouli.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee from the village of Amouli and friend of one of the fishermen who lost his life reported the tragic event. Strong rip currents swept two fishermen at the edge of the reef when surfs were transitioning from high to low tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough stretched across the islands, combined with abundant |moisture and instability converging over American Samoa, to generate a period of heavy rainfall and some flash flooding Thursday June 11th during the late morning and early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local weather office recorded 2.21 inches of rain from 10 AM to 2 PM prompting flash flooding in low lying areas and villages with poor drainage systems. Roads in Ottoville, the iliili golf course, and in the front of SAAB Pago were underwater for most of the day. Several reports from the public and the media included an SUV car stalled due to flooded roadways in the front of LDS State Center in Ottoville, and water filled homes in low-lying areas in the village of Malaeimi. After the event, huge crate-sized potholes were uncovered on the fagaima road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong high pressure system to the south of the islands delivered fresh to strong trades over the territory. This episode enhanced steep seas of 12 to 15 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from the PacIOOS buoy reported surfs over 16 feet with steep wave heights near 12 to 15 feet. This combination along with strong winds caused a small craft to capsize near the auasi port of entry. 15 passengers were onboard and had drifted to sea for nearly 2 hours until help was found. Unfortunately 2 passed away during this incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active surface trough and a building ridge produced strong winds that influenced hazardous rip currents and high surf conditions for the Islands, December 17th to the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large surfs and strong rip currents swept away an unwary fisherman in the village of Vaitogi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Active SPCZ, which formed Tropical Cyclone Zasu as well as YASA produced a week downpour of rain and strong winds for the Islands of American Samoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicle overturns under wet road conditions between the villages of Faganeanea and Matu'u.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Active SPCZ, which formed Tropical Cyclone Zasu as well as YASA produced a week downpour of rain and strong winds for the Islands of American Samoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a week long weather event, WSO Pago Pago finally broke its annual rainfall record since 1981, standing near 165 inches of rain. The new record of 189.90 inches took place during this event. Multiple reports from around the Island of clogged drainages, formation of potholes, and business being affected for the Holidays were called in. Reports of flash flooding in low lying areas were mainly reported in the dense populated districts of Tualauta and Tualatai.
